diff_hunk_content_with_line_number:
[][1] +/*!
[][2] +Defines some helper routines for rejecting invalid Python programs.
[][3] +
[][4] +These routines are named in a way that supports qualified use. For example,
[][5] +`invalid::assignment_targets`.
[][6] +*/
[][7] +
[][8] +use {ruff_python_ast::Expr, ruff_text_size::TextSize};
[][9] +
[][10] +use crate::lexer::{LexicalError, LexicalErrorType};
[][11] +
[][12] +/// Returns an error for invalid assignment targets.
[][13] +///
[][14] +/// # Errors
[][15] +///
[][16] +/// This returns an error when any of the given expressions are themselves
[][17] +/// or contain an expression that is invalid on the left hand side of an
[][18] +/// assignment. For example, all literal expressions are invalid assignment
[][19] +/// targets.
[][20] +pub(crate) fn assignment_targets(targets: &[Expr]) -> Result<(), LexicalError> {
[][21] +    for t in targets {
[][22] +        assignment_target(t)?;
[][23] +    }
[][24] +    Ok(())
[][25] +}
[][26] +
[][27] +/// Returns an error if the given target is invalid for the left hand side of
[][28] +/// an assignment.
[][29] +///
[][30] +/// # Errors
[][31] +///
[][32] +/// This returns an error when the given expression is itself or contains an
[][33] +/// expression that is invalid on the left hand side of an assignment. For
[][34] +/// example, all literal expressions are invalid assignment targets.
[][35] +pub(crate) fn assignment_target(target: &Expr) -> Result<(), LexicalError> {
[][36] +    // Allowing a glob import here because of its limited scope.
[][37] +    #[allow(clippy::enum_glob_use)]
[][38] +    use self::Expr::*;
[][39] +
[][40] +    let err = |location: TextSize| -> LexicalError {
[][41] +        let error = LexicalErrorType::AssignmentError;
[][42] +        LexicalError { error, location }
[][43] +    };
[][44] +    match *target {
[][45] +        BoolOp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][46] +        NamedExpr(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][47] +        BinOp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][48] +        UnaryOp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][49] +        Lambda(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][50] +        IfExp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][51] +        Dict(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][52] +        Set(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][53] +        ListComp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][54] +        SetComp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][55] +        DictComp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][56] +        GeneratorExp(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][57] +        Await(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][58] +        Yield(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][59] +        YieldFrom(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][60] +        Compare(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][61] +        Call(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][62] +        FormattedValue(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][63] +        // FString is recursive, but all its forms are invalid as an
[][64] +        // assignment target, so we can reject it without exploring it.
[][65] +        FString(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][66] +        StringLiteral(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][67] +        BytesLiteral(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][68] +        NumberLiteral(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][69] +        BooleanLiteral(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][70] +        NoneLiteral(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][71] +        EllipsisLiteral(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][72] +        // This isn't in the Python grammar but is Jupyter notebook specific.
[][73] +        // It seems like this should be an error. It does also seem like the
[][74] +        // parser prevents this from ever appearing as an assignment target
[][75] +        // anyway. ---AG
[][76] +        IpyEscapeCommand(ref e) => Err(err(e.range.start())),
[][77] +        // The only nested expressions allowed as an assignment target
[][78] +        // are star exprs, lists and tuples.
[][79] +        Starred(ref e) => assignment_target(&e.value),
[][80] +        List(ref e) => assignment_targets(&e.elts),
[][81] +        Tuple(ref e) => assignment_targets(&e.elts),
[][82] +        // Subscript is recursive and can be invalid, but aren't syntax errors.
[][83] +        // For example, `5[1] = 42` is a type error.
[][84] +        Subscript(_) => Ok(()),
[][85] +        // Similar to Subscript, e.g., `5[1:2] = [42]` is a type error.
[][86] +        Slice(_) => Ok(()),
[][87] +        // Similar to Subscript, e.g., `"foo".y = 42` is an attribute error.
[][88] +        Attribute(_) => Ok(()),
[][89] +        // These are always valid as assignment targets.
[][90] +        Name(_) => Ok(()),
[][91] +    }
[][92] +}
[][93] +
[][94] +#[cfg(test)]
[][95] +mod tests {
[][96] +    use crate::parse_suite;
[][97] +
[][98] +    // First we test, broadly, that various kinds of assignments are now
[][99] +    // rejected by the parser. e.g., `5 = 3`, `5 += 3`, `(5): int = 3`.
[][100] +
[][101] +    // Regression test: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/6895
[][102] +    #[test]
[][103] +    fn err_literal_assignment() {
[][104] +        let ast = parse_suite(r"5 = 3", "<test>");
[][105] +        insta::assert_debug_snapshot!(ast, @r#"
[][106] +Err(
[][107] +    ParseError {
[][108] +        error: Lexical(
[][109] +            AssignmentError,
[][110] +        ),
[][111] +        offset: 0,
[][112] +        source_path: "<test>",
[][113] +    },
[][114] +)
[][115] +"#);
[][116] +    }
[][117] +

[][119] +    // above, but we include it here for good measure.

[][128] +            None,
[][129] +        ),

[][646] +    // This last group of tests checks that assignments we expect to be parsed
[][647] +    // (including some interesting ones) continue to be parsed successfully.
[][648] +
[][649] +    #[test]
[][650] +    fn ok_starred() {
[][651] +        let ast = parse_suite(r"*foo = 42", "<test>");
[][652] +        insta::assert_debug_snapshot!(ast);
[][653] +    }
[][654] +
[][655] +    #[test]

[][659] +    }
[][660] +

[][715] +    // This is a sanity test for what looks like an ipython directive being
[][716] +    // assigned to. Although this doesn't actually parse as an assignment
[][717] +    // statement, but rather, a directive whose value is `foo = 42`.
[][718] +    #[test]
[][719] +    fn ok_ipy_escape_command() {
[][720] +        use crate::Mode;
[][721] +
[][722] +        let src = r"!foo = 42";
[][723] +        let tokens = crate::lexer::lex(src, Mode::Ipython);
[][724] +        let ast = crate::parse_tokens(tokens, src, Mode::Ipython, "<test>");
[][725] +        insta::assert_debug_snapshot!(ast);
[][726] +    }

[][732] +    }
